covered_sections: 4.1 Revision on Right-angled Triangles>Definition 4.1 Right-angled triangle
4.1 Revision on Right-angled Triangles>Conversion of the Pythagoras theorem
4.2 Trigonometric Ratios>Trigonometric values of basic angles
4.2 Trigonometric Ratios>Trigonometric values of angles from a table

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, what is the name of the side opposite the right angle?
questions.hint: Consider which side is always the longest and opposite the \(90^\circ\) angle.
questions.explanation: The side opposite the right angle in a right-angled triangle is called the hypotenuse, which is always the longest side. Option B (adjacent side) is relative to an acute angle, not the right angle itself.

source_trace.content_anchor: The opposite side to the right-angle is called hypotenuse

explanations_tiered.definition: The hypotenuse is the longest side of a right-angled triangle, located opposite the right angle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Every right-angled triangle has exactly one hypotenuse. The hypotenuse is always opposite the \(90^\circ\) angle and is longer than either of the other two sides.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of the hypotenuse as the side that 'hypnotizes' you — it's the longest one, opposite the square corner.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In a right-angled triangle, the hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle. By the Pythagorean theorem, \(h^2 = b^2 + p^2\), so \(h\) is always the largest side.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The hypotenuse is uniquely defined as the side opposite the \(90^\circ\) angle. For any right triangle with legs \(a\) and \(b\), \(c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} > a\) and \(c > b\).
reasoning_steps: Locate the right angle (\(90^\circ\)) in the triangle.
Identify the side that does NOT touch the right angle.
misconceptions: Some students think the hypotenuse is any long side, but it is specifically opposite the right angle.
Confusing adjacent/hypotenuse: the adjacent side touches the acute angle, not opposite the right angle.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Hypotenuse: opposite right angle, longest side, \(c^2 = a^2 + b^2\).

glossary_entry.definition: The side opposite the right angle in a right-angled triangle; it is the longest side.

flashcard.front: What is the name of the side opposite the right angle in a right triangle?
flashcard.back: Hypotenuse. It is the longest side and satisfies \(c^2 = a^2 + b^2\).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which side of a right triangle is opposite the right angle?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: The hypotenuse. It is the longest side.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The side opposite the right angle in a right-angled triangle; always the longest side.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: students misidentify hypotenuse when triangle is rotated. Emphasise 'opposite right angle' not 'bottom side'.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify the hypotenuse in a right-angled triangle
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle, as defined in the note.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: If one acute angle of a right-angled triangle is \(35^\circ\), what is the measure of the other acute angle?
questions.hint: The two acute angles in a right triangle always sum to \(90^\circ\).
questions.explanation: In any right-angled triangle, the two acute angles are complementary, summing to \(90^\circ\). Therefore, \(90^\circ - 35^\circ = 55^\circ\). Option D is the right angle itself, not an acute angle.

source_trace.content_anchor: The sum of the other two interior angles is equal to \(90^\circ\)

explanations_tiered.definition: Complementary angles sum to \(90^\circ\); the acute angles in a right triangle are complementary.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since the right angle is \(90^\circ\), the remaining two angles must sum to \(90^\circ\). Knowing one acute angle allows direct subtraction to find the other.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a right triangle as a corner of a rectangle. The two pointy corners always add up to a square corner — \(90^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Triangle angle sum is \(180^\circ\). Subtract the right angle (\(90^\circ\)) to get \(90^\circ\) for the two acute angles combined. Then \(90^\circ - 35^\circ = 55^\circ\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Let acute angles be \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\). Then \(\alpha + \beta + 90^\circ = 180^\circ \Rightarrow \alpha + \beta = 90^\circ\). Given \(\alpha = 35^\circ\), \(\beta = 55^\circ\).
reasoning_steps: Recall sum of angles in any triangle = \(180^\circ\).
misconceptions: Some students think the two acute angles are equal in every right triangle (only true for isosceles right triangles).
Others mistakenly add \(35^\circ + 90^\circ = 125^\circ\) and subtract from 180 incorrectly.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Acute angles in right triangle sum to \(90^\circ\).

glossary_entry.definition: Two angles whose measures add to \(90^\circ\).

flashcard.front: In a right triangle, one acute angle is \(35^\circ\). Find the other acute angle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: One acute angle of a right triangle is \(35^\circ\). Find the other acute angle.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Two angles whose measures add to \(90^\circ\).
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Acute angles sum to \(90^\circ\) in right triangles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use this to introduce complementary angles before sine/cosine relationship.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Apply angle sum property to find unknown acute angle in a right triangle

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A triangle has side lengths \(5\text{ cm}\), \(12\text{ cm}\), and \(13\text{ cm}\). Which statement is true about this triangle?

options.B: It is right-angled with hypotenuse \(5\text{ cm}\)
options.C: It is right-angled with hypotenuse \(13\text{ cm}\)

questions.hint: Check if \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) using the largest side as \(c\).
questions.explanation: The longest side is \(13\text{ cm}\). \(5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169 = 13^2\), satisfying the Pythagorean theorem. Thus it is right-angled with hypotenuse \(13\text{ cm}\). Option B incorrectly identifies the shortest side as hypotenuse.

explanations_tiered.definition: The converse of the Pythagorean theorem states that if \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), then the triangle is right-angled with hypotenuse \(c\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To verify a right triangle, square all three sides. The largest squared should equal the sum of squares of the other two.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a rope with knots at 3,4,5 — it makes a perfect square corner. Here 5,12,13 works the same way.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Compute \(5^2 + 12^2 = 25 + 144 = 169\). Compute \(13^2 = 169\). Since equality holds, the triangle is right-angled with hypotenuse 13.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By the converse of Pythagoras, if \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) for \(c\) largest, then \(\gamma = 90^\circ\). Here \(c = 13\) satisfies the condition.
misconceptions: Students may use any side as \(c\) without choosing the longest, leading to false negatives.
Some think \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) is only for 3-4-5 triangles; but it works for any right triangle.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Check right triangle: \(c\) longest, \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\).

glossary_entry.definition: If \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\), then the triangle is right-angled with hypotenuse \(c\).

flashcard.front: How can you test if a triangle with sides \(5,12,13\) is right-angled?
flashcard.back: Check \(5^2+12^2 = 13^2\). \(25+144=169\), so yes, right-angled with hypotenuse 13.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Test if a 5-12-13 triangle is right-angled.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: If \(a^2+b^2=c^2\) then the triangle is right-angled.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasise that \(c\) must be the largest side.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Verify right triangles using the converse of Pythagoras theorem

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: A right-angled triangle has legs of length \(6\text{ cm}\) and \(8\text{ cm}\). What is the length of its hypotenuse?
questions.hint: Use the Pythagorean theorem: \(h^2 = b^2 + p^2\).
questions.explanation: By the Pythagorean theorem, \(h^2 = 6^2 + 8^2 = 36 + 64 = 100\). Thus \(h = 10\text{ cm}\) (positive length). Option D results from adding 6+8 without squaring.

explanations_tiered.definition: The Pythagorean theorem states \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\) for the legs \(a,b\) and hypotenuse \(c\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Apply the formula directly. Square each leg, sum them, then take the square root to find the hypotenuse.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a 3-4-5 triangle scaled up: 6-8-10 is double. The hypotenuse is 10.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(h^2 = 6^2 + 8^2 = 36 + 64 = 100\). Taking positive square root gives \(h = 10\text{ cm}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: By Pythagoras, \(c = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2} = \sqrt{36 + 64} = \sqrt{100} = 10\). This is a 3-4-5 triangle scaled by factor 2.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write Pythagorean theorem: \(h^2 = a^2 + b^2\).
reasoning_steps: Identify the legs (6 and 8) and hypotenuse (unknown).
misconceptions: Adding legs without squaring (6+8=14) is a common error.
Forgetting to take square root after summing squares.

glossary_entry.definition: In a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the legs.

flashcard.front: Find the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs 6 cm and 8 cm.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: A right triangle has legs 6 cm and 8 cm. Find the hypotenuse.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Square of hypotenuse = sum of squares of legs.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use 3-4-5 family: 6-8-10 is double. Good pattern recognition.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate hypotenuse length using Pythagoras theorem

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: Which expression equals \(\tan 45^\circ\)?
questions.hint: Recall the ratio for tangent: opposite over adjacent in a 45-45-90 triangle.
questions.explanation: In a 45-45-90 triangle, legs are equal, so opposite/adjacent = 1. Thus \(\tan 45^\circ = 1\). Option C is \(\sin 45^\circ\) or \(\cos 45^\circ\), not tangent.

explanations_tiered.definition: Tangent of an angle in a right triangle is opposite side divided by adjacent side.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a 45-45-90 triangle, both legs are equal, so the ratio opposite/adjacent = 1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In a 45-45-90 triangle, the two pointy sides are the same length, so their ratio is 1.
misconceptions: Confusing tan 45° with sin 45° or cos 45° (both \(1/\sqrt{2}\)).
Thinking tan 45° = 0.5 from pattern sin/cos.

glossary_entry.definition: In a right triangle, tan θ = opposite/adjacent.

flashcard.back: 1. Because opposite/adjacent = 1 in an isosceles right triangle.

publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Recall exact values of trigonometric ratios for basic angles

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.structural_anchor: Example 2 (sin 30, cos 30, tan 30, sin 60, cos 60, tan 60)
questions.question: Which of the following statements is true regarding a 30-60-90 triangle with hypotenuse length 2?
questions.hint: Recall the side ratios: opposite 30° = half hypotenuse, opposite 60° = half hypotenuse × √3.
questions.explanation: In a 30-60-90 triangle, if hypotenuse = 2, side opposite 30° = 1, opposite 60° = √3. Thus cos 30° = adjacent/hypotenuse = √3/2. Option A and B swap the values.

source_trace.structural_element: Example 2 (sin 30, cos 30, tan 30, sin 60, cos 60, tan 60)

explanations_tiered.definition: In a 30-60-90 triangle, sides are proportional to 1 : √3 : 2 opposite 30°, 60°, 90° respectively.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Hypotenuse = 2 → short leg (opposite 30°) = 1, long leg (opposite 60°) = √3. Cos 30° = adjacent/hyp = √3/2.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of an equilateral triangle cut in half: the side next to the 30° angle is the long one, √3 times the short side.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: With hypotenuse 2, side opposite 30° = 1, side opposite 60° = √3. Cos 30° = adjacent/hyp = √3/2.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From geometry, \(30^\circ-60^\circ-90^\circ\) triangle has legs \(x\) and \(x\sqrt{3}\), hypotenuse \(2x\). Here \(2x=2 → x=1\). Adjacent to 30° = \(x\sqrt{3} = \sqrt{3}\).
misconceptions: Confusing opposite/adjacent for 30° and 60° leading to swapped values.
Thinking sin 30° = √3/2 instead of 1/2.

glossary_entry.definition: A right triangle with acute angles 30° and 60°, side ratios 1 : √3 : 2.

flashcard.front: In a 30-60-90 triangle with hypotenuse 2, what is cos 30°?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: In a 30-60-90 triangle with hypotenuse 2, what is cos 30°?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use half-equilateral triangle to derive values.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine trigonometric ratios for 30° and 60° from triangle side ratios
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Hyp=2 → adj=√3, so cos30=√3/2.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Trigonometric values of angles from a table
questions.structural_anchor: Example 1 (using trig table: sin 18°, cos 37°, tan 70°)
questions.question: Using a trigonometric table, the value of \(\sin 18^\circ\) is approximately 0.309017. What is the approximate value of \(\cos 72^\circ\)?
questions.explanation: Since \(\cos(90^\circ - \theta) = \sin \theta\), let \(\theta = 18^\circ\). Then \(\cos 72^\circ = \sin 18^\circ \approx 0.309017\). Other options are unrelated values from the table.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Trigonometric values of angles from a table
source_trace.structural_element: Example 1 (using trig table: sin 18°, cos 37°, tan 70°)

explanations_tiered.definition: Cofunction identity: cosine of an angle equals sine of its complement.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Since 72° and 18° are complementary (sum to 90°), cos 72° = sin 18°.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Cosine and sine are like twins: cos(angle) = sin(its partner angle). Partner of 72° is 18° because 72+18=90.
explanations_tiered.advanced: By definition, \(\cos(90^\circ - \theta) = \sin \theta\) from unit circle symmetry. Applied: \(\cos 72^\circ = \sin 18^\circ\).
worked_solution: Step 2: Apply cofunction identity: cos 72° = sin 18°
misconceptions: Some think cos 72° = sin 72° or cos 18° instead of using complement.
Confusing cofunction with negative angle identities.

glossary_entry.definition: Trigonometric identity relating functions of complementary angles.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Reinforce complement concept before table lookup.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use trigonometric tables and cofunction identities to find values

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.subsection: Trigonometric values of angles from a table
questions.structural_anchor: Example 2 (find angle A given cos A = 0.819152)
questions.question: If \(\cos A^\circ = 0.819152\) according to a trigonometric table, what is the measure of acute angle \(A\)?
questions.hint: Look down the cosine column for 0.819152; read left to get the angle.
questions.explanation: From the trigonometric table, the cosine value 0.819152 corresponds to an angle of \(35^\circ\). Thus \(A = 35^\circ\). The other options yield different cosine values.

source_trace.subsection_anchor: Trigonometric values of angles from a table
source_trace.structural_element: Example 2 (find angle A given cos A = 0.819152)

explanations_tiered.definition: A trigonometric table lists sine, cosine, and tangent values for angles from 0° to 90°.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: To find an angle from a known cosine, locate the cosine value in the table and read the corresponding angle.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Like using a dictionary: find the number 0.819152 in the cosine column, then look left to see the angle (35°).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Scan the cosine column for 0.819152. The entry matches 35°. Therefore A = 35°.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Inverse cosine: \(\cos^{-1}(0.819152) = 35^\circ\). Table lookup approximates inverse trig functions.
worked_solution: Step 1: Find cosine column in table
Step 3: Read angle to the left → 35°
reasoning_steps: Identify the cosine column in the trigonometric table.
misconceptions: Using sine column instead of cosine column.
Reading the value as angle without checking correct row/column.

glossary_entry.definition: A table of precomputed sine, cosine, and tangent values for angles.

flashcard.front: cos A° = 0.819152. Find acute angle A.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Practice inverse table lookup with several values.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Read trigonometric tables in reverse to find angle measures

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: As the measure of an acute angle increases from \(0^\circ\) to \(90^\circ\), which statement correctly describes the behaviour of the sine and cosine functions?
questions.hint: Recall values: sin 0°=0, sin 90°=1; cos 0°=1, cos 90°=0.
questions.explanation: From the table, \(\sin 0^\circ=0\), \(\sin 90^\circ=1\) (increasing), and \(\cos 0^\circ=1\), \(\cos 90^\circ=0\) (decreasing). Thus sine increases while cosine decreases. Option A and B contradict the table.

source_trace.content_anchor: sin increases from 0 to 1, cos decreases from 1 to 0

explanations_tiered.definition: Sine and cosine are trigonometric functions defined for angles in a right triangle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: As angle increases from 0° to 90°, the opposite side grows and adjacent side shrinks relative to hypotenuse, so sine increases, cosine decreases.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a ladder sliding up a wall: the height (sine) goes up, the distance from wall (cosine) goes down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: At 0°, sin=0, cos=1. At 90°, sin=1, cos=0. Intermediate values follow this trend: sine monotonically increases, cosine monotonically decreases.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derivative: \(\frac{d}{d\theta}\sin\theta = \cos\theta > 0\) for \(0<\theta<90°\), and \(\frac{d}{d\theta}\cos\theta = -\sin\theta < 0\). Hence sine ↑, cosine ↓.
misconceptions: Thinking both increase (common confusion with tangent).
Assuming sine decreases because cos increases in some ranges beyond 90°, but restricted to 0-90°.

glossary_entry.definition: A function that consistently increases or decreases over an interval.

flashcard.front: As angle increases from 0° to 90°, does sine increase or decrease? Cosine?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How do sine and cosine behave as the angle increases from 0° to 90°?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Analyse the behaviour of sine and cosine functions over acute angles
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Table 4.1 shows sin 0→1, cos 1→0.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.question: In a right-angled triangle, which condition must hold for the triangle to be isosceles as well?

options.C: The two legs are equal in length
options.D: One leg is twice the other

questions.hint: An isosceles triangle has two equal sides. Which two sides could be equal in a right triangle?
questions.explanation: A right-angled triangle is isosceles if the two legs are equal, making the acute angles \(45^\circ\) each. Hypotenuse cannot equal a leg (it would violate Pythagoras). Option C correctly states leg equality.

source_trace.content_anchor: isosceles right-angled triangle, where the adjacent sides to 90° are equal in length

explanations_tiered.definition: An isosceles right triangle has two equal legs and acute angles of 45°.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For a right triangle to be isosceles, the two legs must be equal. The hypotenuse is always longer, so the only possible equal sides are the legs.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In an isosceles right triangle, the two sides that make the right angle are the same length. Like a square cut diagonally.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Right-angled isosceles triangle: legs equal → angles 45°,45°,90°. Hypotenuse = leg × √2, not equal to leg.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Let legs = \(a\). Then hypotenuse = \(a\sqrt{2}\). For triangle to be isosceles, two sides equal. Legs equal is the only possibility, as hypotenuse is strictly larger.
reasoning_steps: Isosceles requires exactly two equal sides.
Hypotenuse > each leg, so hypotenuse cannot equal a leg.
Therefore the two legs must be equal.
misconceptions: Thinking hypotenuse could equal a leg (impossible by Pythagoras: \(c^2 = a^2 + b^2\), if c = a then b=0).
Assuming any right triangle with a 45° angle is isosceles (true, but condition is legs equal, not angle alone).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Isosceles right triangle → legs equal, angles 45-45-90.

glossary_entry.definition: A right triangle with legs of equal length; acute angles are 45° each.

flashcard.front: What makes a right triangle also isosceles?
flashcard.back: The two legs must be equal in length.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: When is a right triangle also isosceles?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: When the two legs are equal.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Isosceles right triangle: legs equal, hypotenuse = leg·√2.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with 30-60-90 which is not isosceles.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify conditions for an isosceles right triangle
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Two legs equal makes right triangle isosceles (45-45-90).
